nativeRange.habitat: Caves and crevices of lagoon and seaward reefs and drop-offs, typically 8–42 m depth

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and crevices

diet.notes: Generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, and finely chopped meaty foods. Has been documented foraging on tiny worms, copepods, and amphipods from live rock; a productive refugium or pod-populated tank is commonly considered beneficial for long-term success. Newly introduced specimens may need targeted feeding during acclimation.

imageCandidates.notes: Mark Rosenstein; 1400x934. Sharp in-situ reef photo showing all diagnostic features: dark reddish body, white vertical bars, curved white band through the eye, and the characteristic black false eyespots (ocelli) on the dorsal and anal fins near the tail; whole fish in frame.

summary: The White Banded Possum Wrasse is a small, secretive micro-wrasse from the Indo-Pacific, recognized by its distinctive white banding and its habit of sheltering in reef caves and crevices. It is generally considered reef-safe and well suited to peaceful nano reef aquariums with plenty of live rock for cover.
careNotes:
The White Banded Possum Wrasse (Wetmorella albofasciata) is a small, cryptic labrid closely related to the Yellow Banded Possum Wrasse (W. nigropinnata) but distinct in coloration and range.

A minimum tank size of around 75 L (approximately 20 gallons) is commonly cited. The fish tends to be reclusive on introduction, spending its first weeks deep in rockwork, and can struggle to compete for food with bolder tankmates. Aquascaping with abundant caves, overhangs, and shaded crevices is widely considered essential. A refugium (a separate or partitioned section of the system) supporting copepod and amphipod populations is commonly advised for long-term success, as these small crustaceans provide a natural foraging resource.

This species is believed to be a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite, as is typical for wrasses generally. A single male can be kept with multiple females; it is generally advised to introduce females before or simultaneously with the male to avoid aggression. Colors are reported to vary with mood and intensify during courtship.

Like most wrasses, this species has been known to jump, so a tight-fitting lid or fine mesh cover is generally advised. Once acclimated, it gradually tends to become bolder and patrols the water column and cave network.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of this species has not been reliably documented in available aquarium hobby sources. In the wild, the species is known to breed in distinct pairs. Spawning in home aquaria is generally considered unlikely outside of dedicated breeder setups.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product page via wrasse-reef-safe collection. Provided adult size (2.5 in), minimum tank (10–20 gal, effectively 20 gal for species page), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (Easy), reef-safe (yes), diet (carnivore; tiny worms, crustaceans, mysis, frozen, flake), native range (Indonesia), and notes on jumping and harem behavior.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Labridae / Eupercaria misc / Cheilininae), adult max size (6.0 cm TL), broader distribution (East Africa to Hawaiian and Society Islands, south to GBR; Micronesia), wild temperature (24.2–28.2°C), depth (8–42 m), habitat (caves/crevices, lagoon and seaward reefs), and reproduction (oviparous, distinct pairing). Trophic level 3.3. Conservation status Least Concern (2009).

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable captive-breeding accounts have been found in available hobby or scientific sources. The species is known to breed in distinct pairs in the wild.
